8.1 Statistical Data>8.1.2 Graphical presentations of statistical data
8.1 Statistical Data>8.1.3 Measures of central tendency

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If the mean of a dataset is \(\bar{x}\) and each value is increased by a constant \(p\), what happens to the new mean?
questions.hint: Adding the same constant to every data point shifts the mean by that constant.
questions.explanation: When each observation increases by \(p\), the sum increases by \(np\), so the new mean is \(\bar{x} + p\). Option C applies when multiplying each value by a constant, not adding.

source_trace.content_anchor: If each observation is increased by p, the mean of the new observations is (x̄ + p)

explanations_tiered.definition: Adding a constant to every data point increases the mean by that same constant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The mean is a linear operator: \(\text{mean}(x_i + p) = \text{mean}(x_i) + p\). This property makes the mean easy to adjust when data shifts.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If everyone in class gets 5 extra points on a test, the class average also goes up by 5 points. Adding to data adds to the mean.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Let \(\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}\). New data: \(x_i + p\). Sum = \(\sum x_i + np\). New mean = \(\frac{\sum x_i + np}{n} = \bar{x} + p\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The mean is a linear functional: \(\mathbb{E}[X + c] = \mathbb{E}[X] + c\) for expectation. This invariance under translation is essential for normalizing data by subtracting the mean.
reasoning_steps: Recall the mean formula: sum divided by n.
Adding p to each value adds n×p to the total sum.
New sum = old sum + n×p, divide by n gives old mean + p.
misconceptions: Some think adding a constant multiplies the mean (confusing with multiplication property).
Others believe the mean does not change if all values shift equally by the same amount.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Add constant \(p\) to all data: new mean = old mean + \(p\).

glossary_entry.definition: Adding a constant to every observation increases the mean by that constant.

flashcard.front: What happens to the mean when you add 5 to every number in a dataset?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What happens to the mean if you add a constant to every data value?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The mean increases by that same constant: \(\bar{x}_{\text{new}} = \bar{x} + p\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Adding a constant to all data points shifts the mean by that constant.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Mean shifts by added constant: \(\bar{x}_{\text{new}} = \bar{x} + p\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use concrete example: test scores all increased by 2 points.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. The mean increases by exactly the same constant p added to each value.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If a constant \(k\) is added to each value of a dataset, how does the standard deviation change?
questions.hint: Adding the same number to all data points shifts the center but does not change spread.
questions.explanation: Standard deviation measures spread from the mean. Adding a constant shifts both the data and the mean equally, so deviations \((x_i - \bar{x})\) stay the same. Option C applies to multiplication, not addition.

source_trace.content_anchor: If a constant k is added to each value of a data, then the new variance is the same as the old variance. The new standard deviation is also the same as the old.

explanations_tiered.definition: Adding a constant to every data point does not change the standard deviation.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Standard deviation measures dispersion around the mean. Adding a constant shifts both the data and the mean equally, so the distances from the mean remain identical.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If everyone in a race runs 1 second faster, the spread of finish times (how close or far apart runners are) stays the same.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: New data: \(y_i = x_i + k\). New mean: \(\bar{y} = \bar{x} + k\). Deviation: \(y_i - \bar{y} = (x_i + k) - (\bar{x} + k) = x_i - \bar{x}\). Thus variance and standard deviation unchanged.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Standard deviation is translation-invariant because it depends only on deviations from the mean, not on absolute location. This property is crucial for normalization.
reasoning_steps: Write deviation for new data: \( (x_i + k) - (\bar{x} + k)\).
Simplify: \(x_i + k - \bar{x} - k = x_i - \bar{x}\).
Same deviations → same variance → same standard deviation.
misconceptions: Some think adding a constant increases spread because numbers get larger.
Others confuse this with the multiplication property where standard deviation scales by the constant.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Adding constant k: standard deviation unchanged. Spread is location-invariant.

glossary_entry.definition: Adding the same constant to all data values does not change the standard deviation.

flashcard.front: What happens to standard deviation if you add 10 to every number in a dataset?
flashcard.back: Standard deviation remains unchanged. Adding a constant shifts data but not spread.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Grade 9 Mathematics Unit 8 — Standard Deviation Properties
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does adding a constant to all data points change the standard deviation?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No. Standard deviation is unchanged because deviations from the mean stay the same.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A measure of spread that is invariant under addition of a constant to all data values.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Add constant: std dev unchanged. Multiply: std dev scaled.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with multiplication property: multiplying data multiplies std dev by that factor.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Deviations remain the same because mean shifts equally, so standard deviation unchanged.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the correct first step when drawing a histogram from a frequency distribution table?

options.A: Label the marks on the vertical axis
options.C: Draw a horizontal line for the classes
options.D: Choose a scale for the vertical axis

questions.hint: Always start with the axis that will represent the class intervals.
questions.explanation: The first step is drawing a horizontal line to denote the classes. The vertical axis is drawn next, then scales are chosen, and finally bars are constructed. Option A and D come after the horizontal line.

source_trace.content_anchor: Step 1: Draw a horizontal line. This will be where we denote our classes.

explanations_tiered.definition: The first step in drawing a histogram is drawing a horizontal line to denote the class intervals.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A histogram is constructed systematically: horizontal axis (classes), vertical axis (frequency), scaling, then bars. The horizontal line establishes the baseline for class positions.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Before drawing any bars, you need a line where the categories go. Draw the horizontal line first, like a number line for your groups.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Step 1: Draw a horizontal line and place evenly spaced marks for each class interval. The vertical axis is drawn later after the horizontal baseline is established.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Proper histogram construction follows cartesian convention: independent variable (class intervals) on x-axis, dependent variable (frequency) on y-axis. The x-axis base must be drawn first to scale.
reasoning_steps: Identify that the x-axis represents class intervals.
Draw the horizontal axis line.
Mark evenly spaced positions for each class.
Proceed to draw vertical axis.
misconceptions: Some start by labeling the vertical axis without a horizontal base.
Others draw bars before establishing axis scales, leading to misaligned graphs.

glossary_entry.definition: Step 1: Draw a horizontal line and place evenly spaced marks for each class interval.

flashcard.front: What is the first step in drawing a histogram?
flashcard.back: Draw a horizontal line to denote the classes. This establishes where each class interval will be placed.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the correct first step when drawing a histogram?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Draw a horizontal line for the classes. This establishes the baseline for class intervals.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A graphical representation where the first construction step is drawing a horizontal line for classes.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize sequential steps: horizontal axis, vertical axis, scaling, then bars.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Construct a histogram from a frequency distribution
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Step 1 is drawing horizontal line for classes. Then vertical line, then scaling, then bars.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A coin is tossed 1000 times and lands on heads 600 times. What is the experimental probability of getting tails based on this experiment?
questions.hint: Experimental probability = (number of times event occurs) / (total trials).
questions.explanation: Tails occurred \(1000 - 600 = 400\) times. Experimental probability \(= \frac{400}{1000} = 0.4\). Option B is the probability of heads, not tails.

source_trace.content_anchor: If you toss a coin 1,000 times and get a head 600 times and a tail 400 times, then the probability of getting a head is 0.6 and tail is 0.4

explanations_tiered.definition: Experimental probability is the relative frequency of an event: \(\frac{\text{occurrences}}{\text{trials}}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Experimental probability approximates theoretical probability as the number of trials increases (Law of Large Numbers). It is based on observed data, not prior assumptions.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you flip a coin 1000 times and it shows tails 400 times, the experimental probability of tails is 400/1000 = 0.4 — what actually happened.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Experimental probability \(P_{\text{exp}}(\text{tails}) = \frac{400}{1000} = 0.4\). This differs from the theoretical 0.5 due to random variation in a finite number of tosses.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Experimental probability is an estimator of the true probability. Its accuracy improves with sample size; the standard error decreases as \(\sqrt{n}\). Here, \(\hat{p} = 0.4\) with \(n=1000\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Find number of tails: total tosses - heads = 1000 - 600 = 400.
Step 2: Divide tails by total trials: 400/1000 = 0.4.
Step 3: Express as decimal or fraction.
reasoning_steps: Count total number of trials.
Count how many times the event (tails) occurred.
Divide occurrences by total trials.
misconceptions: Some confuse experimental with theoretical probability. Experimental is observed; theoretical is expected.
Computing probability of heads instead of tails is a common reading error.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Experimental probability = observed frequency / total trials.

glossary_entry.definition: The probability of an event determined by conducting an experiment and dividing the number of times the event occurs by the total number of trials.

flashcard.front: A coin tossed 1000 times gives 600 heads. What is experimental probability of tails?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A coin tossed 1000 times gives 600 heads. What is experimental probability of tails?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Probability determined by dividing event frequency by total trials in an experiment.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with theoretical probability (0.5 for a fair coin). Discuss why they differ.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Tails = 1000 - 600 = 400. P = 400/1000 = 0.4. Option B is probability of heads.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 8.1.1 Collection and tabulation of statistical data
questions.question: Which type of data can be meaningfully divided into finer levels and can take almost any numeric value on a scale?
questions.hint: Think about measurements like height, weight, or time.
questions.explanation: Continuous data can be subdivided infinitely and takes any value within a range (e.g., 1.73 m). Discrete data (Option A) consists of countable integers with gaps.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 8.1.1 Collection and tabulation of statistical data
source_trace.content_anchor: Continuous data is information that could be meaningfully divided into finer levels. It can be measured on a scale or continuum and can have almost any numeric value.

explanations_tiered.definition: Continuous data can take any value within a range and can be subdivided into smaller units.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Continuous variables (height, temperature, time) are measured, not counted. They have infinite possible values between any two points, limited only by measurement precision.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Height is continuous: you can be 150 cm, 150.5 cm, or 150.55 cm — there's always a finer measurement. Discrete data is like counting people: you can't have 2.3 people.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Continuous data arises from measurement and can take any value on a scale (e.g., 1.73 m). Discrete data arises from counting and takes only integer values (e.g., 3 children).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Continuous data is typically represented by real numbers within an interval. Its probability distribution is described by a probability density function (PDF), not a probability mass function.
reasoning_steps: Determine if data is measured (continuous) or counted (discrete).
Check if fractional values make sense in context.
If finer subdivisions are always possible, it is continuous.
misconceptions: Some confuse continuous with 'large dataset' — continuity refers to possible values, not quantity.
Rounding or measurement precision does not make continuous data discrete; it is still conceptually continuous.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Continuous data: measured, infinitely divisible (height, weight). Discrete: counted, integers only.

glossary_entry.definition: Data that can be measured on a continuum and can take any numeric value within a range, including fractional values.

flashcard.front: What type of data can be subdivided infinitely and take almost any numeric value?
flashcard.back: Continuous data. Examples: height, weight, temperature, time.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Grade 9 Mathematics Unit 8 — Types of Data
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What type of data can be measured on a scale and take almost any numeric value?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Continuous data. Examples include height, temperature, and time.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Data that can be meaningfully divided into finer levels and measured on a continuous scale.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Continuous = measured, infinitely divisible. Discrete = counted, integers.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Classify data as qualitative, quantitative, discrete, or continuous
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Continuous data can be subdivided infinitely (height, weight). Discrete data (A) consists of separate countable values.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A fair die is rolled once. What is the theoretical probability of rolling an even number?
questions.hint: List the even numbers on a die: 2, 4, 6.
questions.explanation: Even numbers on a die are 2, 4, 6 (3 outcomes). Total outcomes = 6. So \(P = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}\). Option A is probability of a single number, not all evens.

source_trace.content_anchor: theoretical probability of an event E = number of outcomes favorable to E / total number of possible outcomes

explanations_tiered.definition: Theoretical probability is \(\frac{\text{favorable outcomes}}{\text{total outcomes}}\) assuming equally likely outcomes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a fair die, each face has probability \(\frac{1}{6}\). The event "even number" includes three faces, so probability is \(\frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: A die has 6 sides. Even numbers are 2, 4, 6 — that's 3 sides. So the chance of even is 3 out of 6, which simplifies to 1/2.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Theoretical probability assumes a uniform probability measure over the finite sample space. The probability of an event is the ratio of its cardinality to that of the sample space.
worked_solution: Step 2: Identify favorable outcomes: even numbers = \(\{2,4,6\}\), \(n(E) = 3\).
reasoning_steps: List all possible outcomes (sample space).
Count favorable outcomes.
Divide favorable count by total count.

variables.meaning: number of outcomes in sample space

misconceptions: Some count total outcomes incorrectly (e.g., including impossible outcomes).
Others list favorable outcomes as the numbers themselves but forget to count them properly.

glossary_entry.definition: Probability based on equally likely outcomes: favorable outcomes divided by total possible outcomes.

flashcard.front: What is the theoretical probability of rolling an even number on a fair die?
flashcard.back: 1/2. Even numbers: 2,4,6 (3 out of 6 possible outcomes).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is P(even number) when rolling a fair die once?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Probability calculated as ratio of favorable to total outcomes under equally likely assumption.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that 'fair' means equally likely outcomes.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate theoretical probability for simple events
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Favorable: 2,4,6 (3 outcomes). Total: 6. P = 3/6 = 1/2.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If each observation in a dataset is multiplied by a nonzero constant \(c\), the new mean becomes:
questions.hint: Think about scaling: multiplying all numbers by c multiplies the sum and thus the mean.
questions.explanation: If each \(x_i\) becomes \(c x_i\), then the sum becomes \(c \sum x_i\), so the new mean is \(c\bar{x}\). Option A applies to addition, not multiplication.

source_trace.content_anchor: If each observation is multiplied by a nonzero number p, then the mean of the new observations is p x̄

explanations_tiered.definition: Multiplying every data point by a constant multiplies the mean by that same constant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The mean scales linearly: \(\text{mean}(c x_i) = c \cdot \text{mean}(x_i)\). This property is fundamental for unit conversions and data scaling.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If everyone's test score is doubled, the class average also doubles. Multiplying data multiplies the mean.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Let \(\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}\). New data: \(c x_i\). Sum = \(c \sum x_i\). New mean = \(\frac{c \sum x_i}{n} = c \bar{x}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The mean is homogeneous of degree 1: \(\mathbb{E}[cX] = c\mathbb{E}[X]\). This linearity is essential for transformations and expected value calculations.
reasoning_steps: Recall that mean = sum / n.
Multiplying each value by c multiplies the sum by c.
New mean = (c × old sum) / n = c × old mean.
misconceptions: Some think multiplying data adds to the mean (confusing with addition property).
Others forget that the constant must be nonzero; zero would make all data zero.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Multiply all data by c: new mean = c × old mean.

glossary_entry.definition: Multiplying every observation by a constant c multiplies the mean by c.

flashcard.front: What happens to the mean if you multiply every number in a dataset by 3?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: If all data values are multiplied by 5, what happens to the mean?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The mean is also multiplied by 5: \(\bar{x}_{\text{new}} = 5\bar{x}\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Multiplying all observations by a constant multiplies the mean by that constant.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Multiply data by c: mean × c.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with addition property: adding constant shifts mean; multiplying scales mean.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply properties of the mean under linear transformations
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. The sum multiplies by c, so mean multiplies by c. Option A applies to addition, not multiplication.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Two fair coins are tossed simultaneously. How many outcomes are in the sample space?
questions.hint: List all possible ordered pairs: HH, HT, TH, TT.
questions.explanation: Each coin has 2 outcomes. Total outcomes = \(2 \times 2 = 4\). Option D (8) is for three coins; Option A is for one coin.

source_trace.content_anchor: The set of possible outcomes is S = {HH, HT, TH, TT}

explanations_tiered.definition: The sample space for two coins contains 4 equally likely outcomes: HH, HT, TH, TT.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When tossing two coins, order matters because coins are distinct. The multiplication principle gives \(2 \times 2 = 4\) outcomes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Toss a penny and a nickel. Penny can be H or T, nickel can be H or T. That's 2 × 2 = 4 possible pairs.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using the fundamental counting principle: 2 outcomes for first coin × 2 outcomes for second coin = 4 outcomes. Sample space: \(S = \{HH, HT, TH, TT\}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The sample space is the Cartesian product \(\{H,T\} \times \{H,T\}\). Its cardinality is \(2^2 = 4\). Each outcome has probability \(\frac{1}{4}\) for fair coins.
worked_solution: Step 1: First coin: H or T (2 possibilities).
Step 2: Second coin: H or T (2 possibilities).
reasoning_steps: Identify number of outcomes per coin (2).
Multiply outcomes for each independent event.
Result is total sample space size.

formula_analysis.name: Sample space size for k coins

misconceptions: Some list HT only once (ignoring order), giving 3 outcomes: HH, HT, TT.
Others confuse number of coins with number of outcomes per coin.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Two coins: 2 × 2 = 4 outcomes: HH, HT, TH, TT.

glossary_entry.definition: The set of 4 equally likely outcomes when tossing two distinguishable coins: HH, HT, TH, TT.

flashcard.front: How many outcomes are in the sample space when tossing two fair coins?
flashcard.back: 4 outcomes: HH, HT, TH, TT. Each coin has 2 possibilities: 2 × 2 = 4.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Two coins tossed. How many outcomes in sample space?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: 4 outcomes: HH, HT, TH, TT. 2 coins × 2 outcomes each = 4.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment. For two coins, n(S) = 4.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Two coins: 4 outcomes (HH, HT, TH, TT)
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that HT and TH are distinct outcomes because coins are distinct.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine the sample space for simple experiments
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. 2 outcomes per coin → 2 × 2 = 4. Option A is for one coin, D for three coins.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The daily sales (in Birr) at a shop over 5 days are 120, 150, 130, 140, and 160. Calculate the standard deviation (rounded to one decimal place).
questions.hint: Find the mean, then deviations, square them, average, then square root.

source_trace.content_anchor: Standard deviation is the square root of variance

explanations_tiered.definition: Standard deviation is the square root of the mean of squared deviations from the mean.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Standard deviation measures average distance from the mean. For this dataset, the mean is 140, and the typical deviation is about 14.1 Birr.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Find how far each number is from the average, square those distances, average them, then take the square root. This gives a typical 'spread' amount.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Step 1: Mean = 140. Step 2: Deviations: -20,10,-10,0,20. Step 3: Squares: 400,100,100,0,400. Step 4: Average = 1000/5 = 200 (variance). Step 5: σ = √200 ≈ 14.1.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Standard deviation is the root mean square (RMS) deviation. For population data, divide by n; for sample data, divide by n-1 (not used here as this is population).
reasoning_steps: Calculate the mean.
Subtract mean from each value (deviation).
Square each deviation.
Average the squared deviations (variance).
Take square root (standard deviation).
misconceptions: Some forget to square deviations before averaging, leading to zero.
Others take square root of sum of squares without dividing by n first.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: σ = √(mean of squared deviations). For data: mean=140, σ≈14.1.

glossary_entry.definition: A measure of dispersion equal to the square root of the variance.

flashcard.front: Daily sales: 120,150,130,140,160 Birr. What is standard deviation?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Square root of variance; measures typical spread from mean.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Walk through each step. Emphasize squaring to avoid cancellation.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate standard deviation for a data set
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Mean=140, squared deviations sum=1000, variance=200, σ=√200≈14.14≈14.1. Option D is range (40).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A card is drawn at random from a standard deck of 52 cards. What is the probability of drawing a king?
questions.hint: How many kings are in a standard deck?
questions.explanation: There are 4 kings in a 52-card deck. So \(P(\text{king}) = \frac{4}{52} = \frac{1}{13}\). Option A is the probability of a specific card, not any king.

source_trace.content_anchor: P(E) = number of outcomes favorable to E / total number of possible outcomes

explanations_tiered.definition: Probability of drawing a king = number of kings / total cards = 4/52 = 1/13.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In a standard deck, there are 4 suits, each with one king. With 52 equally likely cards, the probability is 4/52 simplified to 1/13.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A standard deck has 4 kings (hearts, diamonds, clubs, spades). So 4 out of 52 cards are kings. That's 1 out of every 13 cards.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Sample space: 52 cards. Event: {K♥, K♦, K♣, K♠} has 4 outcomes. \(P = \frac{4}{52} = \frac{1}{13}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Assuming a well-shuffled deck, each card is equally likely. The probability of drawing a king is the ratio of favorable to total elementary outcomes.
reasoning_steps: Identify total possible outcomes (52 cards).
Count favorable outcomes (4 kings).
Write as simplified fraction.
misconceptions: Some think there is only one king in a deck (confusing with specific card).
Others forget to simplify 4/52 to 1/13.

glossary_entry.definition: The chance of drawing a king from a standard 52-card deck is 1/13.

flashcard.front: What is the probability of drawing a king from a standard 52-card deck?
flashcard.back: 1/13. There are 4 kings and 52 cards, so 4/52 = 1/13.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Probability of drawing a king from a 52-card deck?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Likelihood of an event expressed as a ratio of favorable to total outcomes.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Review that there are 4 suits, each with one king.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate theoretical probability using equally likely outcomes
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. 4 kings out of 52 cards = 4/52 = 1/13. Option A is probability of one specific card, Option C is for a face card (12/52=3/13 not 4/13).
